message:
security(mcp): gate 5 mutating MCP tools via PolicyEngine.authorize (ft-x86z2)

Security audit ft-x86z2 found 8 mutating MCP tools skipping
PolicyEngine.authorize while their peers (wa.send/workflow_run/reserve/
release/accounts_refresh) call it. This closes 5 of them — the 5 whose
tool structs already carry Arc<Config>:

  - wa.tx_run       (mcp_tools.rs:~2678) — gates prepare+commit execute
  - wa.tx_rollback  (:~2902)             — gates compensation phase
  - wa.mission_pause  (:~3985)           — gates state transition
  - wa.mission_resume (:~4109)           — gates state transition
  - wa.mission_abort  (:~4245)           — gates abort decision + persist

New helper mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation at mcp_tools.rs:~177 runs
PolicyEngine.authorize against PolicySurface::Mcp + ActionKind::ExecCommand
(consistent with how wa.events_annotate's audit decision_context already
tags itself). Deny → returns MCP_ERR_POLICY envelope via early-return.
RequireApproval → same envelope with a hint telling the caller to obtain
an allow-once token and retry. Gate fires BEFORE any side effect
(contract load, mission load, state persist).

Intentionally simpler than wa.workflow_run's pattern at :2242 — no
ApprovalStore.attach_to_decision plumbing, since these handlers do not
have an async storage handle in scope at the gate point. Wiring
attach_to_decision (so RequireApproval issues an actionable token) is a
deliberate follow-up once the gate is in place.

The remaining 3 mutating tools — wa.events_annotate/_triage/_label —
have structs with db_path: Arc<PathBuf> and NO config. Adding a gate
requires threading Arc<Config> through their constructors + every call
site (mcp_bridge.rs + ~10 tests). Filed ft-vue5t (P2) as the mechanical
follow-up.

Existing tests continue to pass: default Config yields an
allow-by-default policy decision, so the new early-return branch is
never entered in the default test path. Deny-policy tests per tool are
scoped into a separate pass.

Audit context: mcp is delivered over stdio/localhost by default, so
remote exploitation requires operator opt-in to a networked transport.
The gate closes the trust-boundary-break scenario where a malicious
peer-agent MCP client attempts to abort a mission, rollback a tx, or
pause/resume mission state without any policy evaluation.

Agent: RusticMeadow

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-4qom2","title":"[SEV] tmux cmd_queue cap only enforced at pop site; unbounded growth when state != Idle","description":"frankenterm/mux/src/tmux.rs TmuxDomainState.cmd_queue has CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH=50_000 enforced only inside send_next_command at line 289 via cmd_queue.drain(..excess). send_next_command early-returns at line 286-288 when state != Idle, so the drain-oldest logic never runs if tmux is stuck in State::WaitingForResponse.

Four push sites append to cmd_queue with NO cap check:
- line 196: Event::LayoutChange -> ListAllPanes
- line 223: Event::SessionChanged -> ListCommands
- line 234: Event::WindowAdd -> ListAllWindows
- line 375: split_pane (user split) -> SplitPane

Concrete interleaving: (1) tmux sends a command, enters State::WaitingForResponse; (2) tmux server is slow/hung (real-world scenario: remote tmux on lagging SSH link, tmux server overloaded, Event::Guarded response delayed); (3) GUI user resizes window repeatedly -> stream of Event::LayoutChange -> each push_back at line 196 runs without cap check; (4) send_next_command early-returns because state != Idle. Queue grows unbounded -> memory leak, eventual OOM.

The CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH comment at line 32-33 explicitly advertises 'prevent unbounded memory growth during event storms' as a safety invariant. That invariant is currently only upheld at the pop site, where it can't run under the exact condition (stuck tmux) that event storms exploit.

Fix: extract the cap-enforcement logic into a push_command_capped helper that applies the drain-oldest + warn/error logging at every push site. The helper runs under the cmd_queue lock (just acquired by the caller), so it's atomic with the push and has no additional locking cost.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T00:17:26.601099Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T00:21:03.160715Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T00:21:03.158111Z","close_reason":"Completed: added push_command_capped() helper and routed all 4 production push sites (LayoutChange/SessionChanged/WindowAdd/split_pane) through it. The hard cap is now enforced at push time, not only at pop time — closing the unbounded-growth window when tmux is stuck in State::WaitingForResponse. cargo check passes clean. Commit 1055cffc.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-4r2ib","title":"[mission][dispatch] MissionDispatcher reports accepted dispatch without calling WorkflowRunner::handle_detection","description":"In `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mission_dispatch.rs:200-226`, `dispatch_single()` builds a synthetic detection, then only calls `runner.find_matching_workflow(&detection)` and fabricates `accepted: true` plus a synthetic `execution_id` when a workflow exists. It never invokes `WorkflowRunner::handle_detection()`, so no lock acquisition, no concurrency-limit enforcement, no storage row, and no real workflow start happen.

Impact: callers receive a success-shaped `DispatchResult` that claims work was accepted even though the runner was never asked to start anything.

Fix direction: bridge the sync mission loop to the async runner start path with a bounded runtime, map the actual `WorkflowStartResult` into `DispatchResult`, and only emit started metadata from a real start result.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:09:03.405716Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:10:28.845943Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-4z7vh","title":"RobotCommands::Workflow builds PolicyEngine without require_prompt_active/command_gate/rules — bypasses operator safety config","description":"AUTH BYPASS (robot mode mutations, Severity: Medium).

crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs has 10 PolicyEngine::new call sites. 8 of them wire require_prompt_active from config + .with_command_gate_config(config.safety.command_gate.clone()) + .with_policy_rules(config.safety.rules.clone()). Two do NOT, both under RobotCommands::Workflow:

Site 1 (main.rs:19831, RobotCommands::Workflow::Run):

  let policy_engine = PolicyEngine::new(
      config.safety.rate_limit_per_pane,
      config.safety.rate_limit_global,
      false, // Don't require prompt active for robot mode
  )
  .with_tuning(&config.tuning);

No command_gate, no policy_rules, and require_prompt_active hardcoded false. This engine is fed into CxPolicyInjector → WorkflowRunner; any workflow step that calls send_text (e.g., handle_compaction sending '/compact', handle_usage_limit sending '/refresh') bypasses:
  - the operator's safety.require_prompt_active=true policy,
  - every [[safety.rules]] custom gate,
  - the [safety.command_gate] allow/deny list.

Site 2 (main.rs:20511, RobotCommands::Workflow::Abort): identical construction. abort_execution_with_cx runs compensation steps through the same injector; any send_text in a compensation path bypasses the same three surfaces.

Compare the correctly-gated paths:
  - RobotCommands::Send at main.rs:17652 uses config.safety.require_prompt_active AND .with_command_gate_config + .with_policy_rules.
  - Non-robot workflow invocations at main.rs:21361, 21533, 25051, 27347 all wire the full policy surface.

NOT a duplicate of ft-48ah6 (read-only policy): this bead is specifically about the write/mutation surface (workflow's send_text / command-bearing steps). ft-48ah6 covered the read/search side.

Fix: replace the hardcoded 'false' with config.safety.require_prompt_active at both sites, and chain .with_command_gate_config(config.safety.command_gate.clone()) + .with_policy_rules(config.safety.rules.clone()) before passing the engine to PolicyGatedInjector. Rate-limit args are already correct.

Regression test: a send inside a robot-invoked workflow must be DENIED when config.safety.require_prompt_active=true AND no prompt is active, matching the direct-send gate behavior. Currently it is allowed.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane5","created_at":"2026-04-23T06:46:59.925741Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T07:19:54.084786Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T07:19:54.084480Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 834b59f2. Both RobotCommands::Workflow::Run (main.rs:19831) and ::Abort (main.rs:20520) now build PolicyEngine with config.safety.require_prompt_active and chain .with_command_gate_config + .with_policy_rules — matching the construction at RobotCommands::Send (main.rs:17652) and non-robot workflow sites (21361/21533/25051/27347). Regression test robot_workflow_policy_engine_denies_send_when_prompt_required_ft_4z7vh asserts SendText is denied when require_prompt_active=true + command_running. Test binary compile is currently blocked by pre-existing concurrent-agent breakage (E0603 macro select private, E0063 missing defer_fts_triggers field) unrelated to this fix.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-55l4f","title":"[review][MEDIUM] wa_wait_for.json golden freezes polls:1 — may flake under high-concurrency CI load","description":"In crates/frankenterm-core/tests/golden_robot_envelope/wa_wait_for.json (landed in ecae6caa):

    "polls": 1

The canonicalize fn in crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs only zeroes `now | elapsed_ms | captured_at | *_ms | score | semantic_score`. The `polls` field is not canonicalized.

Under load, this can flake:
- Each wa.wait_for poll spawns a fresh python3 subprocess (fake-wezterm.py) via tokio::Command in crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs:2142-2155.
- On first-poll cache-cold with the swarm running 20+ agents hammering CPU/disk, the subprocess spawn + file read can be slow enough that the poll scheduler issues a second poll before the first returns matching text (build ready matches ready$ immediately on content).
- polls jumps to 2+, golden is polls==1, test fails.

Repro likelihood: low under bench, moderate-to-high under concurrent cargo test + rch + other agent builds hammering I/O.

Fix options (pick one):
1. Canonicalize `polls` to 0 in canonicalize() alongside elapsed_ms/now/captured_at. Pro: stable golden. Con: loses a genuine signal — golden no longer catches regressions where wait_for suddenly needs 10 polls for a match it used to catch on the first try.
2. Split `polls` out of the golden capture: keep it in success_envelope but canonicalize to "polls >= 1" assertion in assert_wait_for_success_data (already there at mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs:~590 — `polls >= 1`), then canonicalize polls to 0 so the golden only sees the structural presence.
3. Accept the flake risk and document that re-running tests clears transient failures.

Option 2 is cleanest: the semantic "polls >= 1" invariant stays enforced; the byte-stable golden covers structure.

Severity: MEDIUM. Not a correctness bug; CI flake risk. Discovered during reviewer audit round 7, 2026-04-23 when verifying ecae6caa (ft-qyw1z+ft-4o5mb CRITICAL fix).

Tag: review, mcp, conformance, flake.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T18:43:30.203619Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T18:47:17.843645Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T18:47:17.843379Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 05c8d36b (Option 2 from the bead). Semantic invariant polls>=1 remains enforced in assert_wait_for_success_data at mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs:474-477 (runs against the live envelope at :733, BEFORE canonicalization). canonicalize() at :480 now zeroes polls alongside now/elapsed_ms/captured_at so the byte-stable golden only freezes structural presence, not scheduler-timing-dependent count. wa_wait_for.json updated to polls:0 to match. Only golden in the tree carrying a polls key is wa_wait_for.json, so the canonicalize rule is unambiguous and cannot mask a real regression in another tool's envelope.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review"]}

 {"id":"ft-b5rbd","title":"[HIGH] GUI resize increment saturation can overflow backend min-size arithmetic","description":"Commit 193a9ebb changes `ResizeIncrementCalculator` to saturate `base_width`/`base_height` at the u16 ceiling (`crates/frankenterm-gui/src/resize_increment_calculator.rs:15-35`). Multiple window backends then immediately compute `base + increment` in u16 without guarding overflow (`frankenterm/window/src/os/macos/window.rs:1410-1412`, `frankenterm/window/src/os/x11/window.rs:1892-1893`, `frankenterm/window/src/os/wayland/window.rs:915-916`). With large padding/border/tab-bar geometry, the new saturation can produce `base_width == u16::MAX`, so `base_width + x` overflows before min-size clamping. This turns an intended overflow hardening change into a runtime panic/wraparound path on oversized layouts. Expected fix: clamp stored base dimensions to `u16::MAX - increment` (or widen backend arithmetic) and add a regression test that asserts `base + increment` stays representable.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T05:58:04.554054Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T06:42:08.945504Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T06:42:08.945232Z","close_reason":"dup of ft-htf0i","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-b677n","title":"[MEDIUM] wgpu readback poll loop has no test coverage for timeout, disconnect, or error-propagation paths","description":"Commit 6f41130c (br-43bbl fix wgpu readback polling) rewrites the
readback wait in crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/webgpu.rs:168-193
from:

\`\`\`rust
self.device.poll(PollType::Wait { submission_index: None, timeout: Some(WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT) })?;
receiver.recv_timeout(WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT)?...
\`\`\`

to a bounded poll loop:

\`\`\`rust
let deadline = Instant::now() + WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT;
loop {
    self.device.poll(wgpu::PollType::Poll)?;
    match receiver.recv_timeout(Duration::from_millis(10)) {
        Ok(result) => { result?; break; }
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Timeout) if Instant::now() < deadline => continue,
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Timeout) => return Err(...),
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Disconnected) => return Err(...),
    }
}
\`\`\`

Concerns with the new implementation:

1. \`PollType::Poll\` is non-blocking by contract in wgpu 25. The loop
   relies on \`Poll\` actually driving the \`map_async\` callback forward.
   If a future wgpu point release changes \`Poll\` to be strictly
   \"dispatch-only\" (no callback invocation), the loop would always
   hit the 5s timeout. There is no test pinning this behavior.

2. The \`Disconnected\` branch is reachable only if the \`map_async\`
   closure drops without sending — that happens e.g., if wgpu drops
   the submission without invoking the callback (device lost, internal
   error). No test exercises this path.

3. \`Instant::now() + WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT\` panics on overflow
   (impossible in practice for 5s, but worth noting as a latent
   concern). Prefer \`checked_add\`.

4. Busy-wait cost: on the timeout path, the loop spins ~500 iterations
   (5000ms / 10ms) calling \`device.poll\` each time. The prior
   \`PollType::Wait\` blocked in the driver. This is a CPU-efficiency
   regression on cancellation/device-loss paths, not a correctness
   regression.

Impact: readback behavior on wgpu failure paths (device lost, long
delay, map_async disconnect) is silently unverified. A correctness
regression in \`PollType::Poll\` semantics between wgpu versions would
silently break screenshots and readback-based features.

Fix direction:
- Add a unit-level test with a mock/stub Device that controls
  \`poll()\` return timing and can force a Disconnected on the mpsc
  channel. Assert each of the four loop outcomes.
- Consider moving the readback loop into a small helper function
  that takes a \`Fn()\` poll closure + channel receiver, to make it
  unit-testable without a real GPU.
- If unit isolation is impractical, at minimum add an integration
  test that triggers a readback and asserts completion within a
  tight CPU budget (regression test for busy-wait blow-up).

Severity: medium — latent risk in a GPU-facing path that typically
works and has no automated coverage for failure modes.

Evidence: commit 6f41130c diff;
crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/webgpu.rs:168-193 at HEAD.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:25:33.419495Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T08:48:13.062749Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T08:48:13.062383Z","close_reason":"Added timeout/disconnect/cancel-style readback coverage and hardened the helper deadline with checked_add","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["gpu","review","test-gap","testing","webgpu"]}
 {"id":"ft-b9ndv","title":"[fuzz] add MCP request params parser harness","description":"Existing fuzz coverage already hits pattern-pack TOML/binary, config TOML, workflow YAML, robot envelope JSON, IPC auth envelope, snapshot deserialization, replay recordings, and term/escape parsers. But there is still no fuzz target for MCP request-param parsing.

Evidence:
- `fuzz/Cargo.toml` has no MCP parser target; current targets stop at `ipc_auth_envelope`, `robot_envelope_json`, and related config parsers.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_types.rs:25-170` defines the externally-controlled MCP request param structs (`StateParams`, `GetTextParams`, `SearchParams`, `EventsParams`, etc.), but they are `pub(super)` and only covered by unit/proptest deserialization checks.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_types.rs:1170-1845` has property tests over many `serde_json::from_value(...)` paths, but no byte-level fuzzing against malformed/truncated/oversized JSON.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_framework.rs:203-265` also round-trips untrusted remote MCP payloads through `serde_json::from_value(...)`.

Why it matters:
- MCP is a network/external-input surface with many optional/defaulted fields and multiple tool-specific param shapes.
- Proptests cover valid structured values, not adversarial raw JSON bytes, duplicate keys, deep nesting, type confusion, or truncation.

Fix:
- Add a fuzz seam plus harness that feeds raw JSON/Value payloads through the MCP request-param decode paths (or framework payload bridge) and asserts no panic / deterministic decode outcomes.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","estimated_minutes":45,"created_at":"2026-04-23T19:26:37.331975Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:57:48.039115Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T19:57:48.038906Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 4561eac1: add MCP request params fuzz harness and seed corpus","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fuzz","review"]}

 {"id":"ft-btg0h","title":"[fuzz] add frankenterm/term Terminal::advance_bytes target for full escape-to-state pipeline","description":"Surface: frankenterm/term/src/terminal.rs:164 — pub fn advance_bytes<B: AsRef<[u8]>>(&mut self, bytes: B). Master terminal state-machine entry point: raw output bytes → frankenterm_escape_parser → Performer::perform (terminalstate/performer.rs:252) → every CSI/SGR/OSC/DCS dispatch path (perform_csi_mode/window/edit/cursor/sgr in terminalstate/mod.rs:1425-2648, plus image pipelines in terminalstate/sixel.rs, terminalstate/kitty.rs (37KB), terminalstate/iterm.rs). Existing escape_parser_raw fuzz target covers only the lexer stage (bytes → Action); termwiz_csi_parser covers the escape-parser CSI roundtrip property. Nothing exercises the state-machine mutations, cursor/edit bounds, or the Sixel/Kitty/iTerm2 image decoders under arbitrary input.

Proposed harness: fuzz/fuzz_targets/term_advance_bytes.rs — construct a Terminal with a minimal TerminalConfiguration impl, 24x80 size, cap input at 256 KB, feed the whole slice through advance_bytes, drop the terminal. No assertions — libFuzzer asserts no-panic / no-OOM / no-unbounded-alloc.

Corpus seeds: plain text, ESC[H clear, SGR sequences, DECSET/DECRST modes, DECRQM, OSC 52 clipboard, OSC 8 hyperlinks, bracketed paste, CSI u keyboard, DCS sixel fragment (\\x1bPq#0;2;100;0;0#0~~\\x1b\\\\), kitty APC image fragment, iTerm2 OSC 1337 file upload preamble, overlong / truncated / interleaved-with-text variants.

 {"id":"ft-fbee5","title":"pattern pack discovery followed symlinks out of tree","description":"# [HIGH] pattern-pack loader: symlink-escape via discover_packs_from_dir

**Subsystem:** patterns / scan_pipeline
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs

## The hole

`load_pack_from_file(path, root)` routes through `sandbox_resolve` only when a
`Some(root)` is supplied. `discover_packs_from_dir`, used for
`.ft/patterns/**` discovery from a workspace root, was calling
`load_pack_from_file(path, None)` for every file + rules.toml it iterated —
so the sandbox check was **skipped** even though the dir itself was
workspace-attacker-controlled.

A hostile repo could ship:

    .ft/patterns/config.yaml -> /Users/victim/.ssh/id_rsa

`path.is_file()` follows the symlink (returns true), `load_pack_from_file`
opens the target, feeds its contents to `serde_yaml::from_str`, and on parse
failure emits:

    tracing::warn!("Skipping invalid user pack {}: {e}", path.display());

Which can echo excerpts of the target file via serde's error reporting
(`mapping expected, found: …`).

## Pre-conditions

* victim clones a hostile repo that contains a symlink under
  `.ft/patterns/`;
* frankenterm loads user packs at session start.

## Fix

`discover_packs_from_dir` now passes `Some(dir)` as the sandbox root to
`load_pack_from_file`. `sandbox_resolve` canonicalizes and rejects any
resolved path outside `dir`. Added regression test
`discover_packs_rejects_symlink_escape`.

## Residual risk

A top-level symlink replacing `.ft/patterns` itself would still escape
because the caller (`load_packs_from_config`) builds `ws_dir =
root_path.join(".ft").join("patterns")` without canonicalizing. That's a
follow-up — tracking separately.

## Sibling work

- 33e84436 security(patterns): add sandbox_resolve helper (helper introduction)
- e8397747 / 8e57c8e0 fix(limits): MAX_PACK_BYTES 16 MiB cap (orthogonal DoS layer)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:10.019601Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.244142Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.243799Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 6ab52765 — canonicalize + in-tree check applied to pack discovery","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ft-05hfm","patterns","security"]}

 {"id":"ft-wajba","title":"tmux cmd_queue drop-head-in-flight corrupts Guarded response routing","description":"frankenterm/mux/src/tmux.rs:73-91 (push_command_capped) vs :165-186 (Guarded response handler).

push_command_capped(queue, cmd) does queue.drain(..excess) when queue exceeds CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH=50_000. The drain is from the FRONT. But when TmuxDomainState.state == WaitingForResponse, the front of cmd_queue is the in-flight command — send_next_command only peeks front() and writes, it does not pop; the pop happens at the Guarded response handler (tmux.rs:172) via cmd_queue.pop_front().

Race / correctness gap: if push_command_capped runs while state == WaitingForResponse and queue.len() reaches 50_001, drain drops the in-flight head. When the Guarded response arrives, pop_front() pops a DIFFERENT pending command and calls cmd.process_result(domain_id, &response) on the wrong command — cross-wiring response payload onto the wrong command parser. Both threads hold the cmd_queue lock so the drain+pop ordering is deterministic under the lock, but the correctness violation stands.

Fix options (not applied here, pattern-level):
 1. When state == WaitingForResponse, skip index 0 in drain: queue.drain(1..=excess) (but preserves head).
 2. Drain from the back (newest-first) which discards most recently enqueued layout/session events that often re-fire.
 3. Abort the in-flight state machine (set state=Idle) and drain — heavy-handed but restores invariant.

Only triggers in pathological event storms (remote tmux lag + 50_000 queued commands) so severity is MEDIUM. The original bug fixed in 1055cffc (push-site cap enforcement) traded unbounded memory for this subtle correctness edge. A regression test would drive tmux into WaitingForResponse, push >50_001 Layout/SessionChanged events, then fire a Guarded response and assert process_result is NOT called against a different command type than the one that was sent.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:16:32.972876Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:41.760204Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:41.759836Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 9cc7e6c2. push_command_capped migrated from free function to TmuxDomainState method so it can consult self.state before choosing drain range:
- state == WaitingForResponse → drain(1..=excess) (preserves index 0, the in-flight cmd awaiting its Guarded response)
- any other state → drain(..excess) (retains existing drop-oldest semantics)

Both drain branches log distinctly so event-storm incidents are traceable. Lock ordering matches Guarded-handler's (cmd_queue first, state transiently inside method): no deadlock. send_next_command's cap-drain is untouched — already guarded by early 'state != Idle → return'.

Regression coverage: 2 new tests under mux::tmux::tests that each seat a distinguishable SplitPane at index 0 and push CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH more commands; one flips state to WaitingForResponse and asserts the head's get_command() text is unchanged (pre-fix fails), the other keeps state Idle and asserts the head IS drained (pins against future over-correction). Existing cmd_queue_hard_cap_drains_oldest_when_exceeded still green after the refactor.

Verification via rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-p5-orch-target:
- cargo check -p mux --lib: clean
- cargo test -p mux --lib cmd_queue: 3/3 green.

 {"id":"ft-wk5fo","title":"move FTS5 indexing off the capture-write transaction","description":"# Background

Follow-up to ft-kcsae (answered). Live capture path in storage.rs
writes synchronously INTO the FTS5 index via three AFTER-triggers:

    storage.rs:169  output_segments_ai AFTER INSERT  → INSERT INTO output_segments_fts
    storage.rs:173  output_segments_ad AFTER DELETE  → fts delete
    storage.rs:177  output_segments_au AFTER UPDATE  → fts delete + insert

Every captured segment pays the inverted-index rebuild cost before
`append_segment_sync` returns (storage.rs:12308 → 12335 INSERT INTO
output_segments). Under 200-pane fanout the trigger cost is paid 200x
per-wire-tick.

# Existing primitives that can be reused

- `fts_pane_progress` table (storage.rs:472) — already tracks per-pane
  last-indexed-seq with resumable offsets.
- `sync_fts_on_startup(conn, FtsSyncConfig)` (storage.rs:16131) — the
  batched catchup engine. Currently only fires at startup.
- `FtsSyncConfig` (storage.rs:2363) — batch_size=100, max_batch_bytes=1
  MB, commit_progress per batch. Ready knobs.

# Proposal

1. Drop the three AFTER triggers on output_segments.
2. Add a writer-thread ticker (every N seconds OR after M segments
   since last catchup) that runs `sync_fts_on_startup` with the
   existing FtsSyncConfig and uses `fts_pane_progress` to pick up
   where it left off.
3. Gate behind a `storage.fts_indexing_mode = 'sync' | 'deferred'`
   config flag; default 'sync' at first, flip to 'deferred' after a
   round of search-freshness observability.
4. Expose catchup-lag (now - max(fts_pane_progress.last_indexed_at))
   as a metric so operators can spot index drift.

# Risk

- Search freshness becomes eventual (default lag = catchup interval).
- FTS rows can briefly diverge from output_segments during a crash
  between INSERT and the next catchup tick — acceptable because
  `sync_fts_on_startup` already handles gap recovery on startup.

# Measurement prerequisite

Before landing: add a bench that measures `append_segment_sync` p99
under a 200-pane load with FTS5 enabled (current) vs. triggers
disabled + deferred catchup. If the gap is not material, don't ship —
the synchronous path is simpler and the trigger tax might be in the
noise given SQLite's WAL + page cache.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"pane1","owner":"pane3-fts5-scaffold","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:00:59.588862Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:28:00.206770Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:28:00.206400Z","close_reason":"Opt-in deferred FTS indexing landed as d74401c6. Added StorageConfig.defer_fts_triggers: bool (default false, preserves current sync behavior). When true, StorageHandle::with_config executes DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS on output_segments_a[iud] immediately after initialize_schema, so new segment writes no longer synchronously rebuild FTS inverted-index pages. Callers are responsible for periodic sync_fts() invocation; the fts_pane_progress + sync_fts_on_startup engine already handles resumable batched catchup. Verified via 4 new regression tests in fts_sync_tests (17 total passing): fts_sync_mode_indexes_on_insert (baseline), fts_deferred_mode_does_not_index_on_insert (post-DROP empty FTS), fts_deferred_mode_catchup_indexes_all_segments (500 segments → catchup → 500 indexed, second catchup is no-op), fts_deferred_mode_catchup_resumes_across_rounds (5 rounds × 100 segments interleaved with catchup, each round indexes exactly 100).  {"id":"ft-x4k2b","title":"[reality-gap] runtime_compat 'audited boundary' scope understated — 709 call sites across 83 files","description":"# Claim (README)

README.md:38-42:

    The runtime model is asupersync-native in the product-facing
    APIs: structured, cancel-correct, and centered on Cx-aware
    orchestration across search, policy, workflows, robot mode,
    and the feature-gated web/distributed surfaces. The remaining
    watch-daemon/runtime startup path still crosses a named
    runtime_compat quarantine while the last task-spawn seams are
    being collapsed.

…and:

    `runtime_compat` remains as an audited boundary for runtime
    lifecycle, channel, time, and blocking semantics; it is not
    the product story.

The framing is: small, named, collapsing.

# Reality (code)

    rg -c "runtime_compat::" crates/frankenterm-core/src/
      → 709 call sites across 83 files

The boundary isn't a narrow interop shim — it's the default way
product code spawns tasks, opens channels, sleeps, and times out.
Every capture loop, every workflow step, every web handler, every
mux-pool operation transits it. The "named quarantine" framing
implies 1-2 modules; the code implies every subsystem.

# Why this matters

- **Trust calibration**: operators and contributors use the README
  to calibrate what's "native asupersync" vs. "transitioning." A
  readme that says "the boundary is small" while 83 files sit on
  it gives the wrong picture of where the project is on its own
  milestone (ft-xbnl0).
- **Cancellation semantics**: asupersync's Cx-first path is
  behaviourally different from runtime_compat's tokio fallback
  (see ft-xbnl0 epic for the formal distinction). Callers reading
  the README might assume their task spawns are Cx-aware when
  they're not.
- **Migration progress tracking**: ft-xbnl0.2.x has been landing
  fixes at the task-spawn level for weeks. The README should track
  which specific paths have crossed the threshold and which are
  still on runtime_compat, so operators can calibrate expectations.

# Recommended disposition

Update README.md:38-42 with either a count ("runtime_compat is
still load-bearing for ~700 call sites across 83 files; the
ft-xbnl0 epic tracks collapse"), or a list of the specific
product surfaces still on runtime_compat (watch daemon,
capture loops, workflow steps, web handlers).

Either form gives operators a calibrated picture without
overstating or understating the state.

# Related

- ft-2guux G1 raised the same concern about the watch daemon
  specifically; closed with a README clarification.
- ft-xbnl0 is the open epic tracking the full migration. This bead
  is specifically about the README framing, not the migration itself.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T20:45:20.507726Z","created_by":"reality-check","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:56.093751Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:50:22.624765Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["docs","reality-gap","runtime"]}
 {"id":"ft-x50ce","title":"[MEDIUM] conformal::ConformalPredictor silently narrows prediction intervals when coverage=NaN","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/conformal.rs:286` — `ConformalPredictor::new` stores `coverage: coverage.clamp(0.01, 0.999)`.

Same `f64::clamp(NaN, _, _) = NaN` gotcha as ft-761tz / ft-b4l62 / ft-jz40p / ft-0p5q5. When `coverage = NaN`, the stored value stays NaN. On every `forecast_all` call:

1. `cal.quantile(self.coverage)` (line 326) is called with NaN coverage.
2. `CalibrationBuffer::quantile` (line 213) computes `k = ((coverage * (n+1)).ceil() as usize).max(1)` → `NaN.ceil() = NaN` → `NaN as usize = 0` → `k = 1`.
3. `k > n` is false (n ≥ 1), so falls through.
4. Returns `sorted[0]` — the **smallest** calibration score.
5. `forecast_all` wraps `(point - q, point + q)` using the smallest q possible — producing the **narrowest** prediction interval the calibration data can support.

**Net**: operators set `coverage = NaN` (via config typo, arithmetic bug upstream, deserialization of an invalid input), and the predictor silently undercuts the formal-coverage guarantee by emitting the tightest possible intervals instead of the widest. Intervals labelled `coverage: NaN` in the output look broken but are still shipped; downstream alerting thresholds driven by these intervals over-fire.

## Severity rationale

- Silent statistical wrong-answer: the predictor's entire point is the formal coverage guarantee; NaN undermines it silently.
- Not a crash or data loss.
- Input reachable via config deserialization or external arithmetic (e.g., `1.0 - alpha` where alpha came from a NaN upstream).

## Fix

Guard at the new() boundary: if `coverage.is_nan()`, snap to the default 0.95 (same as `ConformalConfig::default()` at line 76). Alternative would be to reject and return Result, but callers signature is fn new() -> Self, so keep the infallible default. A tracing::warn log helps debug.

Also tighten `CalibrationBuffer::quantile` at line 213 so even if `self.coverage` is ever NaN (future code paths, direct callers), the function returns None instead of silently-smallest. Same pattern as ft-b4l62/ft-jz40p.

## Tests

* `ft_x50ce_predictor_rejects_nan_coverage_at_construction` — `new(..., coverage: NaN, ..)` stores 0.95 default, not NaN.
* `ft_x50ce_quantile_nan_coverage_returns_none` — `CalibrationBuffer::quantile(NaN)` returns None (defense in depth).

## Scope

Single file (`conformal.rs`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:29:12.968247Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:33:02.964336Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:33:02.963917Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

-{"id":"ft-x86z2","title":"[security][P1] 8 mutating MCP tools skip PolicyEngine.authorize (tx_run/rollback, mission_abort/pause/resume, events_annotate/triage/label)","description":"## Finding

Audit of crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs found 8 mutating MCP tools that do NOT invoke PolicyEngine.authorize, while their peers do.

### Tools with policy gates (grep-verified)
- wa.send (:2009), wa.workflow_run (:2242), wa.reserve (:3127), wa.release (:3259), wa.accounts_refresh (:3499)

### Tools MISSING policy gates
- wa.tx_run (:2607-:2825) — prepare+commit, persists receipts
- wa.tx_rollback (:2825-:2986) — compensation phase
- wa.mission_abort (:4132+) — aborts mission
- wa.mission_pause (:3870+) — persists state transition
- wa.mission_resume (:3994+) — persists state transition
- wa.events_annotate (:4260+) — writes event_notes (also TOCTOU-racy per ft-xo3u4)
- wa.events_triage (:4409+) — writes event_triage_state
- wa.events_label (:4567+) — writes event_labels

## Why this matters

PolicyEngine.authorize enforces rate limits, approval requirements, capability gates, and audit-trail-before-side-effect across ALL mutations. The existing gated tools call it; these 8 skip it. Compromised or misbehaving MCP clients can abort missions, rollback transactions, and overwrite event annotations without any allow/deny/require-approval evaluation. Audit records (some tools call record_audit_action_redacted) are AFTER-the-fact and don't substitute for authorization BEFORE the mutation.

## Fix

Each handler: add engine.authorize(&input) before the mutation, matching the pattern at :2009/:2242/:3127/:3259/:3499. Deny → return MCP_ERR_POLICY_DENIED envelope. RequireApproval → return MCP_ERR_REQUIRE_APPROVAL envelope.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 8 tools gated.
- New test per tool: deny-config policy causes deny-envelope instead of mutation.
- Default allow-policy tests continue to pass.

## Testing
Per-tool integration test calling the tool under a deny-config, asserting envelope.ok=false + appropriate error_code.

## Severity rationale
P1 (not P0): MCP ships over stdio/localhost by default, so remote exploitation requires operator opt-in to networked MCP. But wa.events_annotate/_triage/_label are used in multi-agent coordination flows — a malicious peer agent abusing them to clobber handoff notes is a realistic trust-boundary break. Fix is mechanical.

Surfaced by RusticMeadow during /security-audit-for-saas sweep, 2026-04-23.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:04:41.658155Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:04:41.658155Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","mcp","policy","security"]}

 {"id":"ft-xbnl0","title":"[EPIC] Goal-line closure for FrankenTerm: native Asupersync, zero fake capabilities, and multi-day swarm reliability","description":"# Goal
Close the original FrankenTerm mission all the way to a credible finish line: a swarm-native terminal and control plane that can replace fragile legacy multi-agent terminal workflows, survive large multi-day agent workloads, and stop depending on dishonest capability claims or legacy async runtime debt.

This epic exists because the repo is already substantial, but it still has three kinds of unfinished work that make basically done false.
1. Native async migration is incomplete; direct `tokio`, `smol`, `async-io`, and `runtime_compat` quarantine still shape real behavior.
2. Several supported-path surfaces still advertise capabilities that are stubbed, placeholder-quality, or explicitly `todo!()` / `unimplemented!()`.
3. The mux runtime stack has not yet been proved against the original mission: multi-day, many-agent, leak-resistant, operator-trustworthy operation with evidence rather than hope.

# Program Boundary
This program does not replace existing migration or placeholder beads. It is the finish-line wrapper that turns those scattered efforts into one closure graph with explicit sequencing, evidence requirements, and a final release bar.","design":"# Sequencing
1. Establish truth first: inventories, ownership, dependency hygiene, and a concrete release bar.
2. Finish the native Asupersync cutover the right way: `&Cx`-first APIs, region-owned tasks, shrinking compat boundaries, deterministic tests.
3. Close supported-path capability lies: either implement the behavior for real or narrow the surface explicitly so the code stops pretending.
4. Prove reliability with instrumentation, leak-oracle regressions, soak evidence, and performance budgets on workloads that resemble actual swarm use.
5. Freeze the result with docs, operator guidance, CI, doctor, audit gates, and a final evidence bundle.

# Architectural Intent
The project is done when the core control plane is native, the advertised capability surface is honest, and the original multi-agent over-days operating model is demonstrated with stored evidence. Compile success alone is not a finish line.","acceptance_criteria":"1. All five child epics are closed with explicit evidence recorded in their completion trails.

3. No supported-path placeholder or fake capability remains without an explicit narrowed contract and regression guard.
4. Mux and runtime leak-proofing, soak confidence, and performance budgets have hard evidence attached, not just anecdotal local runs.
5. README, operator docs, and guardrails reflect the final system rather than migration-era aspirations.
6.
